Court documents identify Renteria Hobbs as a known member of the Ham Crazy gang before his killing.

The street gang, known for violent confrontations with other rival gangs in the twin cities, including the St. Paul-based Hit Squad, has operated mostly on the east side since at least 2014 and exploded in popularity after 2020.
Prosecutors have said in previous court filings that the gang's main purpose is to gain and protect their power and reputation on the east side.
To do this, they've used violence and intimidation against rival street gangs.
A long simmering gang war saw Ham Crazy members conspiring to obtain at least a dozen firearms during a three-year period, some stolen and without serial numbers.
They also tried to buy, sell and trade firearms through social media to keep their soldiers well equipped.

These ongoing gang wars resulted in shootings and killings on both sides, endangering the lives of innocent bystanders with many of the crimes taking place at public venues, like the St. Paul bar shooting.

Ham Crazy members were confirmed to be involved in a July 2019 shootout at a live music event in Northeast Minneapolis, exchanging gunshots with rival members of the Free Shots gang, based in North Minneapolis and Rolling 30s Bloods members who are known to operate in South Minneapolis.

Police impounded Glover's car and, after obtaining a search warrant, they examined it for gunshot residue, fingerprints and DNA samples.
Testing identified gunshot residue in the vehicle, which indicated that someone in or nearby shot a firearm, or someone had gunshot residue on them when they got inside.
Forensic specialists matched Glover's fingerprints to prints lifted from the car, but didn't match any to Cole.
Police executed a search warrant at Glover's residence, specifically for clothes he wore during the shooting and for the murder weapon.
At the residence, officers discovered the hat Glover wore the night of the shooting, a loaded Bersa Thunder 380 handgun, wrapped in a rag on a top shelf, and rounds of 9 mm and 22 caliber ammunition.
DNA testing on the grip of the firearm revealed a mixture of DNA from three or more individuals, with Glover's DNA profile as a major contributor.
Notably, a Bersa 380 cannot fire 9 mm rounds like those used in the shooting.
For this reason, the 9 mm ammunition was relevant in finding that Glover possessed the type of firearm used in the murder, a weapon that was never found.
Glover's attorney moved to suppress this evidence, including the Snoopy sweatshirt, arguing that the officers lacked probable cause to believe he had committed a crime.

The defense also moved to suppress the evidence found during the search of his home, including the hat and 9 mm ammunition, arguing that law enforcement misrepresented information in the search warrant application.
After an evidentiary hearing, the district court found that police did have probable cause to arrest Glover based on reasonable inferences drawn from the bar's security footage.

The court also found that the lead investigator did not recklessly misrepresent Glover's interaction with Renteria Hobbs when he used the word confrontation in the search warrant application, because it was a reasonable inference from the bar's security footage.
However, just before the court denied the motion to suppress, Glover's attorney gave notice of what's called alternative perpetrator defense, alleging that Bobby Cole was the shooter.

Glover also moved to introduce evidence of Cole's prior crimes, including a 2017 incident, resulting in Cole's conviction for threats of violence in which Cole kicked in his girlfriend's front door, hit her with his cane, and threatened to have his mom bring a gun over and shoot her.
And a 2019 incident, resulting in Cole's conviction for first-degree aggravated robbery, in which he and an accomplice robbed a victim at gunpoint on the light rail in St. Paul.
For that 2019 incident, Cole was only sentenced to probation.
The court allowed Glover's alternative perpetrator defense, but denied his motion to admit the evidence of Cole's prior crimes, finding they weren't relevant, and the case proceeded to trial.

The court found that Glover didn't have enough evidence to pursue this line of defense, because there were no known suspects from the prior shooting, nor any evidence linking the supposed suspects to the murder.
After the rulings, the prosecution would move to have the case reviewed by a grand jury.
Grand jury proceedings took up much of the summer and fall and rendered a decision in late 2020.
This would be a key turn in the case, as the grand jury opted to indict both Glover and his co-defendant Cole Jr., who was the passenger in the vehicle.
Prosecutors would use the indictment of the passenger to enter into a plea agreement with him, dropping some charges in exchange for an 18-year sentence.
Under Minnesota law, defendants typically serve two-thirds of their sentence, so it equated to roughly 12 years behind bars for Cole.
In the meantime, prosecutors worked hard to build a first-degree premeditated murder case against Glover.
Prosecutors believed Glover to be the shooter in a targeted premeditated murder, but Glover continued to maintain his innocence.

After more than two years elapsed, Glover's case went before a jury in September 2023, with his defense attorney arguing he was not the gunman, but jurors quickly returned a verdict, finding him guilty of first-degree murder and drive-by shooting.
By law, he was automatically sentenced to life in prison in October.
He has the possibility of parole after 30 years.
Generally, first-degree murder in Minnesota requires a life sentence without parole.
However, any murder involving a drive-by shooting automatically constitutes first-degree murder, but also allows for parole after 30 years.
Glover's attorney says he's continued to maintain his innocence, that he didn't pull the trigger.
An appeal was filed in January 2023, but his conviction was upheld in April 2024.
In July 2024, this channel won a request to have the full records released.
Glover has nine prior felony convictions, including three for possession of a firearm by an ineligible person in 2003, 2007 and 2011.
